## External Token Vault vs DBX Native 

Databricks has limitations since it's an analytical and not an operational database. It's for example not possible to enforce constraints which could allow there to be multiple token mappings to the same token.   

Consider this scenario: • Pipeline A updates User 1 with serial_number: 1 -> token -> aaa  
• At the same time, Pipeline B updates User 1 with serial_number: 1 -> token -> aabThis could result in the token vault appearing as follows:  
id   | value | token | type  
1    | user1@gmail.com  | aaa | email 
1    | user1@gmail.com  | aab | email

Workaround: 
- By using appropriate merge statement make sure that we don't get duplicates. Need another join to the database 

### External DB 
**Pros:** 
- Easier to extend with new functionality
- Can be used for other systems also
- Can easy replace parts of the solution like DB if needed since everything is hid under a REST API.
- Provides better isolation

**Cons:** 
- Adds more overhead - Need to deploy an additional DB, REST API and integrate with DBX. 
- Can get help from the data platform team but it created more uncertainty in terms of the deadline

### DBX
**Pros:**  
- Natively integrates with the current ecosystem

**Cons:** 
- Don't have all the functionalities that operational DBs have, for example constraints of uniqueness.
- Locked in DBX
- May require additional effort to expose services to systems outside of Databricks.
- Harder to extend with additional functionality. Harder to do some operations in SQL.






## Workspaces 
#databricks-workspace
https://community.databricks.com/t5/technical-blog/isolation-of-environments-on-the-databricks-data-intelligence/ba-p/56737

All data and resources within the workspace are considered sensitive, eliminating confusion over which permissions apply to which data.
### Risk 
There is a risk that permissions are misconfigured.  
+ Resources may inherit permissions from parent folders or groups. If the parent permissions are too broad, sensitive data may become accessible to unintended users.
- Sometimes, default permissions are set to allow wider access for convenience, which can lead to sensitive data being exposed if not carefully adjusted for each resource. 
- Seperated owners. Admins make mistake it doesn't affect the personal data 
	- **Scenario**: When setting permissions on a sensitive data table, the administrator accidentally grants SELECT permissions to the AllUsers group instead of a specific group.
	• **Consequence**: All users in the workspace can now


# Token Vault Isolation 
+ Apply policies and controls at both the storage level and at the metastore. S3 policies and ADLS ACLs should always be applied using the principle of least-access. Leverage Unity Catalog to apply an additional layer of control over data access.
+ Separate your sensitive data from non-sensitive data both logically and physically; many customers use entirely **separate cloud accounts** (and **Databricks workspaces**) for sensitive and non-sensitive data.
https://www.databricks.com/blog/2022/03/10/functional-workspace-organization-on-databricks.html

# Requirement for tokens: 

## Support updates for deterministic tokens

![[Pasted image 20241003171824.png]]

## Determinstic and Non-determinstic tokens
- Support for non determinstic tokens determinstic tokens. 
https://docs.skyflow.com/tokenization-overview/#skyflow-token-formats

![[Pasted image 20241003170503.png]]

## Preserving token format
https://docs.skyflow.com/tokenization-overview/#skyflow-token-formats
- Format preserving tokens or uuid?
## Configuration of different columns
There should be a definition of different PII column that the user can configure that have its own configuration. 
It should be possible to configure: 
- If it's unique 
- If it's determinstic or non-determinstic 
	- If it's determinstic it's needed to configure which scope it's unique. This will be a set of one or more pii_types. 
- A format preserving regex
- PII_type: name, email etc

This configuration will be stored either as a yaml configuration or in it's own pii_type table with the schema:  id, name, unique, deterministic, scope, format_preserving_regex.  The scope will only be relevant when the token is determinstic. Yaml might be enough. What do you think?


# Token Vault
## Schema
The schema for non determinstic tokens:
	- entity: Could be external user or internal for instance. So for instance MEMBER or INTERNAL.
	- entity_id - unique id associated to  entity. 
	- value - the value to be tokenized 
	- token - the token related to the value 
	- column_name - the column name related to the value that was anonymized 
	- pii_type: the PII type for example, first_name, email, cc_number. Any pii type that is personal data. 
	- created_at - the timestamp it was created
	
The schema for determinstic tokens will be: 
- token
- value
- column_name
- pii_type 
- created_at


## Database
 The database should be a postgres database.

## Endpoint
The user interface with the database by calling a rest api that is using fastapi. 

### Anonymization
The user will anonymize the data by by calling a rest api endpoint /anonymize. The argument to the endpoint is entity_id (which is unique to the user) and the data that should be anonymized and which pii_type and column_name it has. 
When anonymizing it also need to be passed which defined column you're anonymizing which has already be passed. THis is so it's known if the token that is to be anonymized is non-determinstic or determinstic, unqiue etc. 
The flow for anonymizing is: 
1. Check if the token exist 
2. If it does use it. 
3. If it doesn't not, generate a new one. 
4. Return the already generated and the newly generated tokenized values. 

### Deanonymization
There is also an endpoint called /deanonymize 
which takes the entity_id and the column_name that should be deanonymized. For instance specyfying first_name and email will get back the original value from associated to that entity_id.

Seems to be only masking

Looks really old and legacy, now documentation

Looks promising

## Token Reuse
https://docs.informatica.com/content/dam/source/resources/privitar-docs/data-privacy-platform/441/DPP-user/en/privacy-support/removing-token-vault-mapping/important-considerations.html
Once a token has been assigned and the token mapping has been removed, the token will not be reused.

For example, `John` has the ID `12345` which maps to the token `23421`. If this token mapping is removed, the token `23421` will never be reused.

If John re-enters the dataset with the same ID 12345, the ID will be assigned a completely new token.

## Managing audit logs

https://docs.informatica.com/content/dam/source/resources/privitar-docs/data-privacy-platform/441/DPP-user/en/superuser-administration/managing-the-audit-log.html

## Unique identifier 

Direct identifiers such as: Passport Numbers, National Insurance Numbers, Social Security Number, Internal System IDs. 
Quasi-identifiers: Values such as Names, Date of Birth and Country are quasi-identifiers and are not unique.


## Deterministic vs. Non Deterministic tokens:
This refers to whether the same value is always mapped to the same token or if each value is mapped to its own unique token. Deterministic tokenization enables users to lookup exact matches and performs joins between tokenized directly on the columns of a pseudonymized dataset. However, this means that it still leaks some information about original identifiers and consequently reduces the data protection provided by the tokenization engine. For example, it exposes the fact that two different identifiers are the same and, using statistical methods, can be used to reveal the original data.

## Updating Identifiers
The process of only updating referenced identity (e.g., inside the translation/tokens table) instead of updating multiple tables holding a specific token while keeping the token as-is.

### Removal or quasi-identifiers

https://docs.informatica.com/content/dam/source/resources/privitar-docs/data-privacy-platform/441/DPP-user/en/privacy-support/removing-token-vault-mapping/important-considerations.html

## Skyflow 

[https://www.skyflow.com/post/overcoming-the-challenges-and-limitations-of-tokenization](https://www.skyflow.com/post/overcoming-the-challenges-and-limitations-of-tokenization)  
  
Need to account for types when doing deterministic tokens  
How do we generate tokens specific to a user that is non deterministic?  
  
Limit data access  
  
Another major limitation of the traditional tokenization approach is that users and services are either authorized to detokenize data, or they’re not. This means if you have the right to detokenize, you can detokenize any token, even if that token doesn’t belong to you.

![[Pasted image 20240426100052.png]]
![[Pasted image 20240426093702.png]]
 ![[Pasted image 20241120113416.png]]

https://www.skyflow.com/post/demystifying-tokenization-what-every-engineer-should-know
https://www.skyflow.com/post/skyflow-data-types-where-data-privacy-meets-usability
## Distribution

Can use a [mirror](https://docs.posit.co/rspm/admin/python/) to install locally
Install by running:
```
pip install --trusted-host [HOST] --index-url http://[HOST:PORT]/latest/simple PACKAGE-TO-INSTALL
```

It might also be possible to install with [terraform](https://registry.terraform.io/providers/databricks/databricks/latest/docs/resources/library) t

**Do we need to handle partial deletes?**
## Benchmark

### Load testing 22 Apr



--- 
139.520.000 rows took 7.56 minutes (empty token vault though).
139520000 took 7 min to deanonymize.
deanonymnize 128000000 users with 139520000 in token vault took 1.03 minutes with deanonymize_pivot. 1.62 min with normal.
4.000.000 deanonymnize_pivot take 5.52 seconds 
32.000.000 deanonymnize_pivot take 12.79 seconds

23.67 min to anonymize 80 million rows
5 min to anonymize 10.000.000 rows when is empty
13 min to deanonymize_pivot
anonymize 20 million after already inserting 10 million
10 min to anonymize 20 milliion rows (10 already exist in token vault)
27 min to deanonymize_pivot 20 million rows with 20  million already existing. -> 19182181 deanonymized? Why not 20 million? 
2 min deanonymize_pivot 30 million rows

### Reidentification 

When there’s a need for re-identification, the two tables are joined together. What’s interesting here is that the system also allows Mathem to say what granularity of re-identification is needed. For example, the age that was originally 39 is now 40, because the age is rounded to the nearest number divisible by 5. This is in order to reduce the precision so that the data can’t be combined to identify a person (and then be treated as PII).

![[Pasted image 20231212152857.png]]

If Mathem wants to forget someone, then they can simply delete the row for the member in the operational token vault, and then that person can’t be reidentified. They also have the option of deleting certain fields if only parts of the data should be deleted.

### Token  vault

![[Pasted image 20231212153022.png]]

---


![[Pasted image 20231219124348.png]]
 ---
 https://www.piiano.com/blog/pseudonymization-vs-tokenization

[**Tokenization**](https://www.piiano.com/blog/data-tokenization) is the process of substituting a single piece of sensitive information with non-sensitive information. The non-sensitive substitute information is called a token. It can be created using cryptography, a hash function, or a randomly generated index identifier and used to redact the original sensitive information. For example, tokenizing sensitive information like PII or credit card numbers is important when logging them into a file.

The operation of **detokenization**, or translating a token to its corresponding data, should only be done on a need-to-know basis (using the right permissions).To support various use cases, tokenization engines that are used for pseudonymization must account for the following possible requirements:

- **Format-preserving tokens**: These tokens preserve the format of the original data. They are often required in situations with strict storage formatting, such as when changing the database scheme is _impossible_.
- **Deterministic vs. unique tokens**: This refers to whether the same value is always mapped to the same token or if each value is mapped to its own unique token. *Deterministic tokenization* enables users to lookup exact matches and performs joins between tokenized directly on the columns of a pseudonymized dataset. However, this means that it still *leaks some information about original identifiers* and consequently reduces the data protection provided by the tokenization engine. For example, it exposes the fact that two different identifiers are the same and, using statistical methods, can be used to reveal the original data.
- **[Pseudo-Deterministic](https://www.piiano.com/blog/data-tokenization)**: Pseudo-deterministic tokens are designed for unique identifiers. E.g. emails, phones, national-id’s. They are most useful for some PII elements, and anything that the system needs to look up by using the same input value. Example use case: This type of token is required when you get an email address by an end-user logging in to your system. Remember, the email column in the registered users table in the database, has all tokenized values instead of plaintext emails. So first you convert the email to a token (or just search for it) and then you look that up in the database itself.
- **Ephemeral tokens**: These tokens are *valid for a limited amount of time.* Their expiry limits exposure. They are often used for regular tasks, for example, identifiers exported on a nightly job from a transactional system to an analytical data pipeline.
- **Querying the data**: The ability to perform lookups on the tokenized data, such as searching for all emails with the domain “@example.com” in the example above.
- **Efficiently updating/deleting identifiers:** <mark style="background: #FF5582A6;">The process of only updating referenced identity (e.g., inside the translation/tokens table) instead of updating multiple tables holding a specific token while keeping the token as-is.</mark>

### Support for Scalable Data Residency

Skyflow gives you a better option than geo-replicating your entire architecture in each market that has data residency requirements.  
  
Because vaulted tokens aren’t exploitable, they can be safely stored outside of the EU or Brazil without compromising the personal data of EU or Brazilian residents (which remains safely within those jurisdictions).

![[Pasted image 20231204181610.png]]

---

![[Pasted image 20231121170340.png]]


https://medium.com/snowflake/an-exploration-of-the-vgs-vault-tokenizer-snowflake-native-app-b170e030813
Tokenization on the other hand allows access keys to be rotated on whatever frequency is specified by an agency’s security team without any additional operations being required on the previously-tokenized data.

After data is tokenized in the VGS vault, only users or processes who are explicitly provided permissions can reveal the original data.

Tiers of users 

- **Level 3**: This tier represents any non-sensitive data in the data lake. This may include first names or tokenized values. When Acme Bank analysts who only have access to Level 3 data query tables, any sensitive data is presented to them in a tokenized format.
- **Level 2**: This tier applies to any customer contact information. This may be a customer’s email, phone number, or shipping address. However, users who can access Level 2 do NOT have access to government-issued id numbers. In this example, Acme Bank chose to grant this level of access to the “Senior Analyst” role in Snowflake.
- **Level 1**: This tier is for any high-risk data in the system. Users who are granted access to Level 1 data are trusted to see the original un-tokenized values for all customer information, including government-issued IDs. At Acme Bank, this level of access is only granted to the “Data Owners” role in Snowflake.

![[Pasted image 20231121171605.png]]

In Acme Bank’s situation, the data team decided that it was important for analyst users (Level 3) to be able to see the last 4 digits of the original SSN. This was implemented by selecting the FPE_ACC_NUM_T_FOUR alias format for that column in the VGS Vault Tokenizer app. The tokens created with this format will preserve the last 4 digits of the account number while keeping the token numeric.

## Adding Tokenization to a Data Pipeline
In the previous examples, Acme Bank was concerned about tokenizing data that was already in their Snowflake environment. But what about new data coming in from their vendors and partners?

The solution: **Staging Tokenization**.

The VGS Vault Tokenizer app can be configured to allow data to be securely imported into a staging table. The data will then be picked up by the VGS Vault Tokenizer, tokenized, and moved into a target table where it can be used by the users who query the data for business information.

### Tokenization format
https://www.skyflow.com/post/demystifying-tokenization-what-every-engineer-should-know

Some storage and transmission systems, such as APIs, have an expectation that the data they work with is in a certain format. For example, a database system might expect that the “phone_number” column follows the E.164 standard for international numbers. If a phone number is tokenized as a random number or UUID, the token can’t be stored in the database, breaking the existing infrastructure.

In this section, we walk you through a variety of different tokenization approaches and what features they are designed to enable.

#### Format-preserving Tokenization
For example, an 11-digit phone number could be exchanged for a randomly generated 11-digit number following the same format that the database expects to store phone numbers in. Or an email address could be exchanged for a randomly generated string that respects the email format: the username prefix followed by the “@” symbol and a domain (i.e., _username@domain.com_).

### Length-Preserving Tokenization
On the other hand, an email address requires a format-preserving token, but not necessarily a length-preserving token.


### Random and Consistent Tokenization
If you wanted the same feature with tokenization, you would choose to generate _random tokens_ (sometimes called _non-deterministic tokens_). With random tokens, tokenizing the same value twice will yield two different tokens. The table above might end up looking like the one below, with the **First Name** column replaced with random tokens.

However, there are use cases where having a different token value for the same plaintext value is not desirable. You might want to be able to **search the tokenized data store for a record where the first name is equal to “Donald” or join on the tokenized first name. A random-token approach can’t support these operations.**

In this case, you need a _consistent_ tokenization method (sometimes called ‘deterministic tokenization” because tokens might be generated by a deterministic process). This guarantees that tokenizing “Donald” will always return the same token. Here’s how the original table would look if you consistently tokenize the **First Name** column. 

![](https://assets-global.website-files.com/5fff1b18d19a56869649c806/6298f7d4619827a12ff800b7_8sIJDwnC_JZXVmt2DAnbVdFTlmyMVw4Pix1cgZvyBz8HRF6J4_3vVrJqzDGg7ArVMLhshloYrhqtP0MUOWLDujgeO-li7u1tS4cfPyy6Gu4xVRcK-Zxbm27F9MDWdMpxMRII4ipcSMIBBp4G-w.png)


https://www.piiano.com/blog/data-tokenization


#### Value Tokens and Cell Tokens
Instead of creating a token for the first name “Donald”, we can create a token that points to a specific cell: in this case, the **First Name** field of the user record being added. These are _cell_ _tokens_ – they don’t point to the value, but refer to the storage location. If needed, we can still have tokens maintain some of the above constraints like having a fixed length or a specific format.

Cell tokens enable certain optimizations — for example, if a customer’s phone number changes, then just the cell referenced by the token needs to be updated. The token itself doesn’t change, which frees you from the need to update every tokenized data store when token values change or are deleted.

_Decision Tree for Determining the Type of Token You Have_
![[Pasted image 20231121172951.png]]

#### Consider Entropy and Space Size

The size of the output space (i.e., how many characters are available to encode the token) puts an upper bound on the [entropy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entropy_(information_theory)) of the tokens. Entropy is an important concept in security since it determines how secure your data is (all other factors being the same, more entropy implies better security). Ideally, you want to maximize entropy, but there is a tradeoff between token entropy and application compatibility. There might be application requirements that impact the token format and constraints. 

A crucial thing to note is that certain formats greatly restrict the space from which a tokenization process can pick tokens. For example, if we want tokens to be 10 digits long, as in the format-preserving tokens for phone numbers, then there can only be 10^10 (or 10 billion) tokens – i.e. the space for the token is all 10 digit numbers.

### Token generatoin logic
![[Pasted image 20231212135354.png]]

### Restrict Detokenization Permissions

Obviously, if you replace your sensitive data with tokens, and allow anyone to “redeem” (or detokenize) the token, then you haven’t really improved your data security! You should always use the Principle of Least Privilege when granting users or services access to a detokenization service. Ideally, you should utilize [data governance](https://www.skyflow.com/post/introducing-the-skyflow-data-governance-engine) to control access. Most applications don’t need to detokenize data, so those applications shouldn’t have permissions to do so – they should just work with the tokens as-is.

https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/best-practices-policy-tags
![[Pasted image 20240108094023.png]]

https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/column-level-security-intro#example_use_case
![[Pasted image 20240108094109.png]]


### Rate Limit and Monitor

Rate limiting and monitoring help you catch potential abuse scenarios, like the bruteforce attack mentioned earlier. For example, if a service is trying to detokenize a lot of tokens that don’t exist, then this could be suspicious behavior. You want to have monitoring set up to alert you about situations like this.


 https://freedium.cfd/https://ketansoni.medium.com/data-tokenization-9c5aa213cd71

### Statistical controls
#statistics-controls
In some cases, systems will also perform statistical tests to mitigate the risk of re-identification. This could include analysis of any incoming dataset to ensure its addition to the de-identified database/environment will not, in combination with any other dataset which already exists in the environment, put data subjects at risk of identification.

### Technical  implementation

- Mirror tokenization for fallback 


#### Pseudo code

+ What happens if we change database implementation? Can we develop it so it's easy to change. 

Lookup to table vs rest api vs cache and lookup. Can we design the solution so the code doesn't need to change significantly. 
What happens if we change the database? 
Can we use a registry pattern so you can register there own anonymization functions to a certain pattern. For instance for email retain the suffix. 



---

Tokenization  

Data tokenization involves the substitution of sensitive data elements, such as personally identifiable information (PII), with randomly generated tokens with no inherent meaning.  

Unlike encryption, where data can be decrypted with the right key, tokens have no direct relationship with the original data and cannot be reversed to reveal the original information without access to a secure tokenization system. 

The tokenization process involves a Token Vault that manages the mapping between the original sensitive data and its corresponding tokens. In the image below there is you can see that there’s a mapping for the user 4c9711c8-240b-46f0-bdfb-4a290e0a8d17 between the real email [user1@gmail.com](mailto:user1@gmail.com) and the fake email [sdfkjj@gmail.com](mailto:sdfkjj@gmail.com). You can also notice that the user id 4c9711c8-240b-46f0-bdfb-4a290e0a8d17 is a token to the real user id 317b0890-e6c6-4ab7-91da-3d840f4a82a6. 

![test](data:image/png;base64,) 

Anonymizing incoming data  

Anonymizing incoming data involves two distinct scenarios: either the PII value is absent in the Token Vault, or it is already present. 

![](data:image/png;base64,) 

Scenario 1 – The value is absent  

In this scenario, the PII value is novel, representing a new customer or new customer details. Consequently, a new token must be generated for this specific PII value. The generated token, along with its association to the original value, is subsequently inserted into the token vault. Once the new token is created, it can be employed to substitute the original value, thereby achieving data anonymization. 

![](data:image/png;base64,) 

Scenario 2 – The value is already present Given that the PII value is already present, and its corresponding token is stored in the token vault, the user id and PII value can be used as a “key” which is used to find the corresponding token value and substitute the PII value. 

![](data:image/png;base64,) 

Deanonymization  

To retrieve the actual PII values associated with the provided token the same lookup is performed as in scenario 2, but this time using the id and token as “key”. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)  

Tokenization in a Multi-Cloud Environment 

Each cloud provider, Azure and AWS, will have its own dedicated data vault, ensuring complete isolation between them. This setup eliminates the necessity to synchronize the data vaults, mitigating the risk of data discrepancies. Consequently, when transferring data from AWS to Azure, a two-step process is implemented: the data is first deanonymized before being sent to Azure, and subsequently anonymized once again. 

PII Taxonomy & Data access levels  

A PII Taxonomy can be used to divide PII data into different tiers dependent on the sensitivity of the data. Each of the PII types falls into one of the top-level categories. For example, the High category contains Credit card, Government ID, and Biometric.  

![](data:image/png;base64,) 

Establishing a PII Taxonomy creates a structured approach to govern the PII data a 

- Granting access to an entire group of PII types at once. 
    
- Moving PII types from one tier to another. For example, you can move Address from the Low tier to the Medium tier to further restrict its access, without needing to update all access policies to that column. For example, if there were multiple policies granting access to the Address column, they all need to be updated if it was decided that it falls into a different tier/category.  
    

After creating a Taxonomy, a data steward can establish distinct data access levels by forming groups that align with each category. As an illustration, a group named "high-tier-access" can be created to align with the High access category.

---

### Entity 
https://validio.io/blog/pseudonymization-of-pii-in-streaming-with-mathem

## Scout 24 meeting 04-19

Here are some questions we’d like to discuss: 
- What was the specific data model?
- How is the tokenization package / service used?
- How is data accessed?
- Details about tokenization:
- Are tokens unique?
- Is the process format preserving?
- Timeline? How long did it take?


3 months 

Aws 
Query engine presto (a lot of tweaking) - trino (due to requirement changes) - starburst
AWS emer cluster with spark, aws data pieplines own  wrapper -> migrating away due to  
s3 storage, parquet, delta
airflow orchestrating and step functions

Analytics clients: 
- MIicrostrategy
- SQL client in datactalog
- Jupyter notebooks like EMR


Customer -> revenue 
customer -> listing/real estate 
users/seekers -> behavioural data (impressions, navigation)
customers - landlords, homeowners

customers and users are users and are PII. 
emails, addresses


Challenges 
- regular GDPR deletion s3 at our scale are costly, slow 
- would break data + kpi consisetnency - 50 million revenue and that users are removed. 
- We want to reduce altering data to minimum
- no metadata 
- little data ownership
- Everybody responsability to be GDPR complient

Build vs buy 
tokenization vs encryption

DPI - direct personal identifier
product purchase - not direclty but not identifying by itself. 
![[Pasted image 20240418102729.png]]

## Learnings 
### WE REALISED THAT IDENTIFYING USE CASE IS 80%  
It isn't as simple as scaling infrastructure. 
Join with the massive amount of data and the mapping table. 
Need to look at which data use cases you have.  

- Correct metadata is critical, not only for flagging pii, business descriptions and fuel the data catalog 
- **data ownership is critical establish accountability and transparency - have a hard part with it. We need to monitor and observe it and react it** 
- Changing requirements? step back and review your approach 
- Amount of identifying use case is more is relying on identifying 
- Make sure you have reliable numbers on cost for: 
	- implementation 
	- running the system
	- deletion of data
	
Delta format it's easier to delete the data.
Timeline: 8 months were not enough. Take your time! 

Update identifier to delete


Also consider identifier as PII 
Also consider excternal data 

Anonymize data will fuel 80% data.

data without dpi - joined to mapping which contain person master data. 


pseudo combines with metadata yaml in github. 
EIther it direct it with DPI or it it anonymized it if it contains.
We don't want to be the bottleneck so therefore they rely on the contract

![[Pasted image 20240418103922.png]]
#### Hard way how to educate people
Might not be enough with documents 

Good to have legal onboard

DPO doesn't need to rely on the DPO but at the leader board. If you don't have have support from upper management it's not gonna happen. 


petabytes scale. 
2 hours of deletion with delta. 
We also looked into iceberg but fall between the cracks due to the deadline. 

#### Focus on deadline 
We want to have a deadline on a certain date. If you get late in the project you omit other aspects. We didn't focus on how to access the data (deanoynmize).  It might be a breaking change. And joining the huge amount of data. 
STEP BACK! After 1 year they should have asked themself if they're right thing to do. Be courageous enough to say NO. 

Create a mapping table. Replace 123 with ABC and when you're done delete this mapping table. 

Batch
